author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon
book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

metadata.author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon

title: Canon XVIII.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The crime of conspiracy or banding together is utterly prohibited even by the secular law, and much more ought it to be forbidden in the Church of God.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Therefore, if any, whether clergymen or monks, should be detected in conspiring or banding together, or hatching plots against their bishops or fellow-clergy, they shall by all means be deposed from their own rank.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Ancient Epitome of Canon XVIII.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Clerics and Monks, if they shall have dared to hold conventicles and to conspire against the bishop, shall be cast out of their rank.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In order to appreciate this canon, we must consider the case of Ibas bishop of Edessa.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: He had been attached to the Nestorians, but after the reunion between Cyril and John of Antioch had re-entered into communion with Cyril on the ground that Cyril had explained his anathemas (Mansi, vii., 240), or, as he wrote to Maris (in a letter famous as one of the “Three Chapters”) that God had “softened the Egyptian’s heart” (ib., 248).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Four of his priests (Samuel, Cyrus, Maras, and Eulegius), stimulated, says Fleury (xxvij.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 19) by Uranius bishop of Himeria, accused Ibas of Nestorianism before his patriarch Domnus of Antioch, who held a synod, but, as Samuel and Cyrus failed to appear, pronounced them defaulters and set aside the case (Mansi, vii.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: They went up to Constantinople, and persuaded Theodosius and archbishop Flavian to appoint a commission for inquiring into the matter.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Two sessions, so to speak, were held by the three prelates thus appointed, one at Berytus the other at Tyre.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: At Berytus, according to the extant minutes (Mansi, vii., 212 ff.), five new accusers joined the original four, and charges were brought which affected the moral character of Ibas as well as his orthodoxy.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The charge of having used a “blasphemous” speech implying that Christ was but a man deified, was rebutted by a statement signed by some sixty clerics of Edessa, who according to the accusers, had been present when Ibas uttered it.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: At Tyre the episcopal judges succeeded in making peace, and accusers and accused partook of the communion together (ib., vii., 209).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The sequence of these proceedings cannot be thoroughly ascertained, but Hefele (sect.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 169) agrees with Tillemont (xv., 474 et seqq.) in dating the trial at Berytus slightly earlier than that at Tyre, and assigning both to the February of 448 or 449.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Fleury inverts this order, and thinks that, “notwithstanding the reconciliation” at Tyre, the four accusers renewed their prosecution of Ibas (xxvij.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 20); but he has to suppose two applications on their part to Theodosius and Flavian, which seems improbable.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: “The Council is believed,” says Tillemont (xv., 698), “to have had this case in mind when drawing up the present canon:” and one can hardly help thinking that, on a spot within sight of Constantinople, they must have recalled the protracted sufferings which malignant plotters had inflicted on St. Chrysostom.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This canon is found in part in the Corpus Juris Canonici, Gratian’s Decretum, Pars II., Causa XI., Quæst.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: I., canons xxj. and xxiij.
